posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Seems like it is a large majority are now including stems in these recent warmer vintages to keep some freshness in the wines, unless the harvest of a particular cuvee is too large for a producer to add the stems into their vat of choice.

I wouldn’t say a large majority by any means, though certainly quite a few of the more famous domaines are.

While stems may bring the perception of freshness, potassium in the stems actually precipitates out tartaric acid and increases the pH of the wine. Some producers who used 100% whole bunch in years like 2018 and 2019, and who didn’t add tartaric acid to compensate, ended up with pHs close to 4.0…

For this reason, some producers who often use a lot of stems, such as d’Arlot, are sometimes destemming more.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Seems like it is a large majority are now including stems in these recent warmer vintages to keep some freshness in the wines, unless the harvest of a particular cuvee is too large for a producer to add the stems into their vat of choice.

While stems may bring the perception of freshness, potassium in the stems actually precipitates out tartaric acid and increases the pH of the wine. Some producers who used 100% whole bunch in years like 2018 and 2019, and who didn’t add tartaric acid to compensate, ended up with pHs close to 4.0…

This is what I thought but I stayed silent for change, seeing how many sources claim they don’t destem because of the freshness.

I’ve noticed that whole bunches might add fragrance, complexity and savory qualities and I know they can help with things such as fermentation thermoregulation and punchdowns, but I’ve never though they’d add any freshness. On the contrary, I’ve thought they’re a good way to soften up wines in cooler vintages by bringing the pH up a bit, if they are high in acidity. Furthermore, in cooler vintages the stems have more time to lignify. Although I’ve understood people pay too much attention to woody stems and one can use stems that still are quite green without any worries, in hotter (ie. early) vintages the stems might be still a bit too unripe to benefit the wine.

On the contrary, I’ve thought they’re a good way to soften up wines in cooler vintages by bringing the pH up a bit, if they are high in acidity. Furthermore, in cooler vintages the stems have more time to lignify. Although I’ve understood people pay too much attention to woody stems and one can use stems that still are quite green without any worries, in hotter (ie. early) vintages the stems might be still a bit too unripe to benefit the wine.

Yeah, it is a very complex subject and I think that, in some cases, other considerations such as choice of oak, timing of malolactic fermentation, duration of élevage post-malo, extent of lees retention, etc can contribute to some of the aromas that people tend to associate with whole bunch vinification. Much as a lot of the technique behind white Burgundy production can often get over-simplified to “reductive wine making”, so a lot of the technique behind the reds gets reduced to destemmed or whole bunch.

Sure, that’s indeed the fashionable discourse of today; but the fact that many producers who de-stem make make very “fresh” wines complicates things. Do Mugnier’s entirely destemmed wines, for example, lack freshness (or perfume, for that matter)? I wouldn’t say so.

I think on the one hand, it’s a technique that some influential people work with; and, in these ripe vintages, there’s not much obvious downside to playing around with stems. On the other hand, it’s a talking point that’s very easy to explain to the general public—and, for journalists, perhaps I’m being cynical, but it sometimes seems like great filler to take up space in a tasting note: by the time you’ve devoted fifty words to the vine age, location of the parcel, percentage of stems, and percentage of new oak, then there’s less pressure to actually evoke the wine’s structure or aromas.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The other technique that’s of interest here is what I think is best described in French as “vendange entière decoupé”, i.e. “cut up whole bunches”, which was pioneered by Leroy around the turn of the millennium I believe. Essentially, you cut out the rachis, leaving the berries attached to the pedicels. I do this for my Chambolle-Musigny, and I do think in a sense it combines the best of both worlds in that you get some of the powdery stem tannin (but not too much) and whole cluster-like perfume, without the astringency or potassium that can come from the stems. Here are some photos of a cluster, the detritus left over (including rigorous sorting, not that it was honestly really necessary in 2020, but… one only makes wine once a year), and the end result. NB that the berries that are not red are either Muscat or Pinot Gris, both of which are planted in small quantities in the old vine parcel we draw on.


2020 Chambolle cluster by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


2020 Chambolle detritus by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


2020 Chambolle decoupé by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

This is becoming quite fashionable in Burgundy, and the wines’ prices now justify the labor. But I do think it’s more than just a talking point.

FYI, the grapes in this photo came in at around 13.6% natural alcohol, pH 3.30, with under two grams per liter malic acid.

I think the term ‘freshness’ as it pertains to stems is a bit ‘confusing’ indeed. There are many winemakers who disdain the use of stems for they feel it detracts from the purity of fruit a wine may have.

I do agree with this - which is why I DO everything 100% whole cluster. It is much easier to make fruit forward wines, IMHO, than it is to.make wines that have the ‘additional non-fruit elements’ that stems naturally bring to the table.

The biggest challenge with stems is that you’re not certain what you’ll ‘get’ in any given year, but that’s part of the ‘fun’, right?

Cheers.

I have absolutely no idea how to make wines or the process it takes so bare with my dumb question.
Is it possible to add stems little by little during the wine making process to get better results?

Here’s the challenge - define 'better?

We all know that some consumers disdain even the smallest whiff of stems whereas others really dig it.

I don’t think that there is a ‘best’ way to make a wine - a winemaker has to just set a course and move forward with he or she is the best expression of those grapes from that vineyard or block for that vintage . . .

Cheers

I phrased it incorrectly. Is it possible to add more stems later on in the wine making process to achieve winemaker’s idea of what he/she wants for that vintage?.or you can only add the grapes and stems one time and have to make the best of what is put in?

No … but… I have heard of vignerons who destem, keep the stems and add a certain amount back into a fermentation for various reasons (a couple of interviews on “I’ll Drink to That” podcast). They would assess the early fermentation and add a bit. Given the logistics, this would only work if a wine maker has grapes arriving at different times (e.g., block A is harvested and destemmed on day 1, block b is harvested on day 4 and then these stems could theoretically be used in the block A fermentation bins). I’m in Oregon and haven’t heard of anyone doing this in our part of the world— it was an odd thing I heard on a podcast interview. Not the norm.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
One thing not yet mentioned in this discussion that sort of blew my mind when I found out: wines fermented with 100% whole clusters convert sugars into lower alcohols, roughly 1% less alcohol in the finished wines versus a destemmed wine…those who are chemistry informed can feel free to chime in with why that is.
Certainly amidst the ongoing warmer-than-it-ever-was trends, this method would seem indispensible in an effort to make more balanced, lower octane wines.

What I prefer : 100% with stem as it is more complex as it aged.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Why they soak up alcohol and color is for others who are into the science to discuss. Where’s Otto?

My ears are burning.

I haven’t really dug into this subject, but off the top of my head I’d guess the stems could dilute the must. After all, they contain water (which is pumped into grapes) but no sugar, so I can imagine if the stem is a few percent of the total weight of a grape bunch, the water that is inside the stems is going to have a minuscule, but still perhaps noticeable effect on the final ABV level.

I don’t know if the stems could affect the whole fermentation process, making the yeast struggle, making it require more sugar to produce one % of alcohol (ie. making less alcohol from a set amount of sugar). From what I’ve understood, the stems actually facilitate the fermentation process. Go figure.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: My unproven guess is they soak up the alcohol at the end of fermentation, so if you did extended post fermentative maceration you would/could/might decrease the percentge of alcohol.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
My unproven guess is they soak up the alcohol at the end of fermentation, so if you did extended post fermentative maceration you would/could/might decrease the percentge of alcohol.

I’m not sure if that’s chemically sound. That would mean that there was something in the stems that binds alcohol and keeps it from staying in the must.

It’s different how the stems can soak up color. That’s because anthocyanins can actually bind with the stems, coloring them, thus leaching coloring pigments from the must. However, the same thing doesn’t seem to happen with ethanol.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
I’m not sure why it is not possible for them to ‘soak up’ some alcohol seeing that stems are porous. And I would agree that perhaps extended maceration, when the alcohol level is the highest it will be, may cause more of this phenomenon than if you press earlier. It’s anyone’s guess, right?

The point is that they can soak up all kinds of stuff, including alcohol. But unless there is a mechanism that binds alcohol, they will soak up all the stuff from the must - not only alcohol, but also water and other molecules small enough to fit into those those pores. Since a water molecule is much smaller than an alcohol molecule, it would be plausible for water to permeate the stems better than alcohol and thus increase the ABV! But that doesn’t seem to happen. So could you explain why / how the stems would soak up just alcohol but not the water in which the alcohol is? Like I said earlier, it doesn’t sound plausible from a chemical point of view. More likely the stems affect the fermentation process.

If one had means to check out accurately the ABV in wine, it would be easy to check out if the stems soaked up any alcohol. Just check out the ABV in a finished, dry wine, grab a jar, fill it with stems and then pour up to the brim with wine. Seal the jar airtight. After a couple of weeks check out if the ABV has dropped.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
Why they soak up alcohol and color is for others who are into the science to discuss. Where’s Otto?

My ears are burning.

I haven’t really dug into this subject, but off the top of my head I’d guess the stems could dilute the must. After all, they contain water (which is pumped into grapes) but no sugar, so I can imagine if the stem is a few percent of the total weight of a grape bunch, the water that is inside the stems is going to have a minuscule, but still perhaps noticeable effect on the final ABV level.

I don’t know if the stems could affect the whole fermentation process, making the yeast struggle, making it require more sugar to produce one % of alcohol (ie. making less alcohol from a set amount of sugar). From what I’ve understood, the stems actually facilitate the fermentation process. Go figure.

Yes, this is what I learned: the stems release enough water to make a lower ABV. My experience is that it drops ABV by 0.5% (my Colette Pinot Noir is 100% destemmed; Ariane 100% whole cluster) plus or minus 0.2 depending on the year.

In terms of facilitating fermentation, there are a couple of things going on. First, there’s a fair bit of whole berry integrity in a whole cluster fermentation (some wine makers are famously agressive in foot treading (DRC ‘whole body treading — interns in speedos!), I only go in to my knees and this leaves lots of whole berries. Some favor pump overs leaving even more whole berries than treading methods). These whole berries will contribute carbonic macération, an anaerobic enzymatic fermentation which brings the freshness that folks where wondering about (bright, candied fruit flavors). The whole clusters also create lots of small air pockets during the first few days especially and to your point Otto, this helps facilitate the saccharomyces fermentation. But oxygen can also pump up the organisms that cause VA (acetic acid bacteria, hanseniaspora, etc) and EA too, so it’s a fine line. I like to have a pied de cuve (starter culture) from the vineyard ready to go on day 1 to avoid any VA and EA issues.

Back to the point about whole berries. At the end of what I call “free run fermentation,” when we go to press, a fair amount of whole berries will release their sugars as part of the press fraction. About 1 to 2 Brix has been my experience and so these sugars will finish fermenting in barrel. Yet another factor that can affect flavor (longer, slower fermentations capture more aromatics in the wine matrix, is one line of thought).

In terms of the pH question raised earlier, we have low potassium soils in the Willamette Valley (compared to a dry climate soil) and I usually see an increase of only 0.05pH. I have not had to add tartaric acid in my whole clusters because the juice starts in the 3.3 pH zone (that’s the Eola-Amity Hills terroir for you) and even the final WC wine stays below 3.4 if you can believe that. (I had my doubts initially.) Cheers,

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked:
I will single-handedly probably keep this thread in play in coming days, as I am picking my first Pinot next week. Fascinating topic. After reading many articles on the subject, and also this thread, and trying many Pinots (not Burgs) made both ways, here are all the thoughts I can’t stop thinking about regarding stem inclusion and my intellectual difficulty embracing it at the moment. I would love people’s thoughts on these points. And remember… I am coming from making Bordeaux varietals the last 17 years, so don’t get offended!!!..it’s just I often find the best way to get enlightened answers is to be a bit of a Devil’s Advocate now and then.

My difficulty in wanting whole bunch (or stem inclusion) seems to be…

  1. I want to ferment fruit, not vegetables.
  2. If I am adding stems to make a wine more complex, why not add all sorts of other “non-fruit” things too?
  3. It is easier to punch down de-stemmed fruit and thus get better extraction
  4. Stems raise pH (I don’t want my Pinots to have the pH of Napa Cab)
  5. I suspect the aromatic lift from stems is more noticeable when wines are young, than older.
  6. VA sometimes rises more in whole bunch ferments due to air pockets in the fermentation vessel.
  7. Stems don’t lignify in Cali until 25 brix and even DRC’s stems look green in videos I’ve seen. “Ripe stems” seems a bit of a myth to me.
  8. I’ve never chewed on a stem at 23.5 brix and thought it tasted good.
  9. Don’t like the loss of color. Why is loss of color now considered somehow good?
  10. Much harder to get the textures one can achieve when de-stemmed ferments reach 90-93F, vs. whole cluster ferments at 75-83F.
  11. Higher pH with stems often requires acid additions as well… so you’ve now added TWO things that are not fruit.
  12. How can stems make a big difference in aromatics when some of those same wines get 50-100% new oak?
  13. Ripe seeds in my experience create more “spice” than stems do.
  14. Reduction and lees contact create more interesting aromatic notes than stems do.
  15. If stems help slow fermentation, can’t that be achieved better by glycol jackets in tanks?

All this being said, I plan to ferment one-half with 20% stems and one with 0% to see for myself. Alas, that probably will just create a “confirmation bias”, as what can be learned in one vintage? Making Cab is just so much easier and more profitable that I have probably lost my mind going down this rabbit hole, but I simply have to scratch this itch. Thanks ahead of time for your thoughts…

But how do we know they would not be even better without stems? Here would be a possible retort…

1978 Jayer Cros-Parantoux $19,431 current price (Premier Cru destemmed)

1978 DRC Richebourg $5750, Grand Cru, with stems. This vineyard is adjacent to Cros-P and thus a better comparison.

It’s not persuasive to say that stems “cause” DRC and Leroy to be more expensive (or better) unless a side by side is done comparing the same vineyard and same vintage done both ways by the same winemaker. Otherwise there are too many confounding variables. It may be that DRC is the best because of terroir and not stems.

All this being said, I plan to ferment one-half with 20% stems and one with 0% to see for myself. Alas, that probably will just create a “confirmation bias”, as what can be learned in one vintage? Making Cab is just so much easier and more profitable that I have probably lost my mind going down this rabbit hole, but I simply have to scratch this itch. Thanks ahead of time for your thoughts…

Roy,

You should make the wine the way you want to make it and go from there.

Destemmed and whole cluster are not a binary one way or the other. I did destemmed ferments in my first year, then started playing with whole cluster. It was almost 10 years before I stopped doing any destemmed ferments, although I was down to 1-3 destemmed fermenters in 3-4 years.

Ultimately, for me most wines made from destemmed fruit are just more boring than their counterparts. Since I taste the wines routinely for 20 months, being bored by them isn’t a good choice(IMO, and specific to WV Pinot Noir in my cellars). While I have liked quite a few bottles of Pinot Noir made with destemmed fruit, the large majority of my favorite Pinot Noirs from Oregon, Burgundy, or elsewhere come from ferments with stems in them. You should do what you like though.

Your list of hypotheticals is a good list of fun thoughts.

  1. which vegetables have you fermented to know that you don’t want to ferment vegetables? Not that stems have anything to do with vegetables. Vegetables are a storage for carbohydrates, like fruit but not as sweet. Stems are the plant itself, and a storehouse for the astringencies that are also necessary for making a vinous beverage vs. alcoholic fruit punch.

  2. because stems are part the vine and the place, and they work. You could add many other non-fruit items…you know, like barrels, yeast, egg whites, enzymes(not a recommendation), yada-yada-yada.

  3. destemmed punch downs are WAY easier. I will definitely go back to destemmed fruit when I am old and have lost my palate for nuance in wines.

  4. I routinely rebalance pH in my wines after the shift in pH from stems. And as of yet have not veen struck by lightning for doing so, nor did I need to sign a contract with any maleficient entity offering my soul in return for the ability to acidify a ferment.

  5. great thought. Use of stems will increase the intensity of the dumb phase(you have more tannins tying up the fruit), but IMO as the wines age the polymerization of stem related compounds adds significantly to the density and complex nature of the wines. However, these are primarily non-fruit aromatics and flavors. But the extra tannins do more than just assist the wines durability.

  6. yes. You would need to manage VA closely and the wines will be close to threshold regardless.

  7. stems at the 45th parallel lignify in every vintage that I have made, regardless of the color of the stem. The seasonal change between summer solstice and fall equinox provides the vines with a very clear message that winter is on the way. The keys to successful use of stems, from a vegetal perspective, in the Willamette Valley is to avoid using enzymes, keep peak ferment temps between 85-90F, kepp potential alcohols below 14%, and keep ferments below 3 tons.

I’ve used significant amounts of stems in every vintage since 2003, and I do not have vegetal results. The wines lock down and are low fruit, sometimes for more years than is fiscally intelligent, but they’re not vegetal.


That said, I have had plenty of CA Pinot Noirs with modest amounts of stems that show green notes. I would guess(just a guess) that the diurnal temperature shifts created by fog, do not have the same effect as the more distinct shift in sun hours to influence lignification(which doesn’t actually require browning to have occurred.)

  1. I’ve never chewed on black peppercorns and thought that they tasted good. But I still use quite a bit of black pepper on my food…

  2. why is the retention of color considered good? I do not care in the slightest about color, but if darker color matters to you then destemming is the smart choice. Especially if you are looking for more extraction overall.

  3. meh…we reach 83-90F most years in 1.5 ton fermenters. If you want 90-93, make sure your YANs are good or warm the room up a bit. (Edit: at 20% stems you won’t see a significant difference in your ferment temps. Maybe a degree or so. And you can get similar texture at 91F or 92F.)

  4. tartaric acid is the main acid naturally found in grapes. Both tartaric and bunch stems are far more of the fruit than oak barrels. Before I give you your merit badge for purity, are you foregoing elevage in barrel?

  5. magic

  6. not my experience by I don’t work with Bordeaux varietals or CA fruit, and will defer to your expertise with them.

  7. why limit yourself to a simple reduction/lees contact or stems, when you can easily have it all. That said, saying that reduction is in the same plane of nuance as stems, is like saying that AC/DC is more complex than Prince.

  8. No. (IMO) Glycol jackets only cool the exterior of ferments, rather than throughout the fermentation. Getting that cooling to the interior of the ferment is an inefficient process. And they require a significant use of energy to do so, and do a rudimentary/poor job of cooling ferments(in comparison to simply adding stems.

I would concur that you won’t learn much from two ferments in one year, but I hope you have a lot of fun doing it. Maybe you will learn that you just need to keep doing it! (And with Cab there to pay the bills, why not?)

Best of luck,

Marcus

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I will single-handedly probably keep this thread in play in coming days, as I am picking my first Pinot next week. Fascinating topic. After reading many articles on the subject, and also this thread, and trying many Pinots (not Burgs) made both ways, here are all the thoughts I can’t stop thinking about regarding stem inclusion and my intellectual difficulty embracing it at the moment. I would love people’s thoughts on these points. And remember… I am coming from making Bordeaux varietals the last 17 years, so don’t get offended!!!..it’s just I often find the best way to get enlightened answers is to be a bit of a Devil’s Advocate now and then.

My difficulty in wanting whole bunch (or stem inclusion) seems to be…

  1. I want to ferment fruit, not vegetables.
  2. If I am adding stems to make a wine more complex, why not add all sorts of other “non-fruit” things too?
  3. It is easier to punch down de-stemmed fruit and thus get better extraction
  4. Stems raise pH (I don’t want my Pinots to have the pH of Napa Cab)
  5. I suspect the aromatic lift from stems is more noticeable when wines are young, than older.
  6. VA sometimes rises more in whole bunch ferments due to air pockets in the fermentation vessel.
  7. Stems don’t lignify in Cali until 25 brix and even DRC’s stems look green in videos I’ve seen. “Ripe stems” seems a bit of a myth to me.
  8. I’ve never chewed on a stem at 23.5 brix and thought it tasted good.
  9. Don’t like the loss of color. Why is loss of color now considered somehow good?
  10. Much harder to get the textures one can achieve when de-stemmed ferments reach 90-93F, vs. whole cluster ferments at 75-83F.
  11. Higher pH with stems often requires acid additions as well… so you’ve now added TWO things that are not fruit.
  12. How can stems make a big difference in aromatics when some of those same wines get 50-100% new oak?
  13. Ripe seeds in my experience create more “spice” than stems do.
  14. Reduction and lees contact create more interesting aromatic notes than stems do.
  15. If stems help slow fermentation, can’t that be achieved better by glycol jackets in tanks?

All this being said, I plan to ferment one-half with 20% stems and one with 0% to see for myself. Alas, that probably will just create a “confirmation bias”, as what can be learned in one vintage? Making Cab is just so much easier and more profitable that I have probably lost my mind going down this rabbit hole, but I simply have to scratch this itch. Thanks ahead of time for your thoughts…

1a) Some world-class Cabs have stem inclusion.
1b) Many world-class Cabs are prized for including vegetal character in the mix. (Note to internet whack-jobs: This is not response 1a.)
4) Yes. And some Pinot vineyards this year have come in with pH unusually low, with good flavor development and normal brix, so some winemakers are choosing stem inclusion for their first time. Anyways, it’s a choice that fits some sites and clones well, and not others.
7) Pinot needs to be handled gently, regardless. A harsh destemmer can give you more undesirable green than gentle whole cluster.
8) I’ve tasted Pinot stems (in the 23 range) that weren’t harsh or green at all.
12) There’s a lot of shitty Pinot out there. I’d recommend tasting a lot of Pinots made from your source to see what you think. But, with stem inclusion I’d lean toward zero new oak, and without probably not want to go over 20%, if that, and with something unobtrusive and supportive like Billon.
15) The stylistic choice is usually to favor aromatics and lessen fruit, not make a “big” wine.
16) It’s a good idea to press a little early, which avoids some of the issues/concerns you have to a great degree.
17) My experience working with multiple Pinot producers, as well as working with dozens of grape varieties, from all over the state, from Paso to Manton, and Marin to the Sierra Foothills, is something like stem inclusion is something to have in the tool box. You’ll see some top CA and Burg producers vary their usage by wine and vintage.
18) Have you tried big Basin Pinots? That’s someone who started out making big Syrah. When they started making Pinots they went moderate ripeness on them. They are neither wimpy or “big”, but quite good. They’ve continued to evolve, and I haven’t kept up. I heard they were playing with whole cluster a big, but not sure if that included with Pinot… I bring them up because I think their wines fit your preferences.

I am 55 years old now. Hopefully I figure out what I like before I am dead.

In reality, even my Cabernet winemaking is a continual evolution. It’s what makes it so much fun.

To answer an earlier question, three winemakers who fully destem are Steve Kistler (Occidental), Helen Turley (Marcassin) and Thomas Brown (for his Summa and Platt Rivers-Marie Pinots,) which are his most sought after. So there a few heavyweights who don’t use stems.

I chatted with TRB about this subject this morning and he gave me permission to say the following. He says he prefers stems for riper Pinots because the stems take the edge off flavors being too fruity, but in his cooler vineyards he disliked the potential green character, so he de-stems. What I find interesting is that his de-stemmed Pinots taste and smell like they have some stems, but they don’t. And his Occidental Ridge, which has some stems, is by far his most fruitful.

posts.name: Bruce G.
posts.cooked:
I haven’t really dug into this subject, but off the top of my head I’d guess the stems could dilute the must. After all, they contain water (which is pumped into grapes) but no sugar, so I can imagine if the stem is a few percent of the total weight of a grape bunch, the water that is inside the stems is going to have a minuscule, but still perhaps noticeable effect on the final ABV level.

I imagine its this.
A classmate of mine did his thesis on the effects of whole cluster PN on fermentation, and found three main results: lower alcohol, lower color, and higher pH.
The lower alcohol was due to dilution of the must by the low-sugar liquid contained in the stems. Stems at harvest do contain some sugar, but far less than grapes.
Lower color was because of some anthocyanin adhesion to the stems.
And higher pH was due to the higher potassium in the stems, leading to a higher amount of tartrate precipitation.

This was back in the early 80s, so you’d think that the matter was settled.
With wine, though, we continually re-visit these things (with good cause).

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I had forgotten to post the reply from JP Guyon’s oenologist, who gave a thougtful reply to the question of why less alcohol in whole cluster ferments (google translate edited, I tried to clean it up a bit):
"For whole cluster vinifications, there are several factors that will come into play:

  • a solid / liquid part different from the destemmed, with a more substantial transfer of ethanol from the liquid to the solids in the whole cluster and conversely a transfer of “sap” from the stalk to the liquid. This already creates a slight alcohol loss (-0.1 / 0.3% vol depending on the vintage and slight dilution effect (-0.1% vol)

  • Alcoholic fermentation in itself is different in terms of yeast metabolism. The whole cluster vinification will induce a longer and more intense FGP (glycerol-pyruvic fermentation), with the use of sugar which will not only be dedicated to the production of ethanol but will be partly redirected towards glycerol type by-products ( among others)

  • The ferments themselves will be different between the same destemmed vinified grape and whole cluster. The stalk carries a significant amount of microorganisms. The successions of strains are not quite the same and this is to the detriment, generally, of the production of alcohol.

All put together, we regularly arrive at a differential of around -0.5% vol.

This still corresponds to 8-9 g / L of sugar which is not directed to the production of ethanol."

Voilà.
Santé

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked: Randy Dunn once said in an interview “one of the best Cabs” he ever made had some whole clusters. But given Cab and Cab Franc have pyrazines in the grapes in rather high quantities until a certain level of ripeness, adding even more “green” in the form of stems seems to have little qualitative upside in my mind. posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
One topic that hasn’t come up is that whole cluster fermentations require more fermentation vessel space. When speaking to a burgundy producer at IPNC a few years ago, she was very open to say that space limitations prevented whole cluster fermentations at their estate, especially in warmer vintages with higher yields (which would classically be the exact times to make more whole cluster).

I make both 100% whole cluster (Ariane) and 100% destemmed (Colette) Pinot Noir (with Marie-Paule being a blend of the two). Same vineyard, very different wines. My original plan was to make less whole cluster on cooler vintages (due to less stem oxidation; by the way, the term “lignification” is misused, all mature plant tissue has lignification; the browning of stems is due to oxidation). But I am really, really liking my cooler climate whole cluster. It sings on many levels, changes dramatically after bottling (in a good way), and isn’t showing any green harshness or bitterness. To one of Marcus’ points, the destemmed Pinot is more predictable and well, boring from a wine maker’s point of view, but it does get lots of love from clients and critics.

Great post Larry.

IMO, destemmed wines are more fruit driven, and if that’s the goal then destemmed is the way to go.

But I feel that with modern techniques and warmer vintages, fruit is hardly lacking in most wines and often is beyond what I would consider balanced.

I also feel that the diiferences in site are rarely limited to fruit flavors and if the “volume” on the fruit is too high, the other notes often get lost. While stem components can also dominate a wines expression, they do act as a way of reining fruit in. Both through counterbalancing the sweetness of fruit with bitter, and by binding up fruit components in youth. And allowing those components back into the wine years later helping to elevate a wines mature expression.

It’s really important to note that while whole cluster wines can be enjoyable in youth, the main goal is a to elevate a later phase in the wine’s evolution, and capturing the early fruit with tannin to make it available later on.

It showed the Montebello subregion quite well, which I love, and was compelling and inspiring.

Research, intuition, experimentation and results are good guides. We never did wc with PN because it didn’t fit with Bryan’s vision, and I don’t feel any of those (5-6/year, then down to two) PNs would’ve been better with it. But, we certainly utilized it with other grapes to good effect, imo, with the sites’ character readily apparent. Then, Rhys is well known for a good portion of stem inclusion, but they quickly determined a couple of the clones at Alpine didn’t well with it, so those got 100% destemmed from then on. But, those are components of a mostly wc wine.

Some grapes ask for it, others would suffer for it, and some can go either way. So, my view is do it when you should, not because you can.

Interesting that destemmed is more popular early on. How do you think the dynamic changes over time? I have some 2017 Taillepieds from both Marquis d’Angerville and de Montille to run my own comparative studies on in a decade-plus, so would wonder what ages I should start tucking in?

It will be interesting to hear about your side-by-side on the Taillepieds. I would want to start checking in at age 5 (just had a 2013 PN last week that was past its prime and was glad to have tasted the 5 year version which was superb — and was real surprised it had lost its brightness). Other winemaking differences may be a factor in your comparison. My first questions would be: native (longer) fermentations or not; percent, if any, new oak barrels for each producer; then élevage time in barrel (16 mos each producer?). There are others but those would be my top three.

So when I say “early on,” I am referring to the first two years (or so) after bottling. My whole cluster wines really evolve more during this period than my destemmed wines; then things level out and start the longer, slower bottle aging changes…. We sell a lot of wine upon release so this can make whole cluster a bit more challenging to the American palate. I really need to hold off more on the release of the Ariane whole cluster Pinot!

Can you give sources on this? Because at least I’ve seen Jamie Goode discuss all the time about stem lignification and I’d think he knows what he’s talking about, having PhD in plant biology and all.

To me, wood oxidation sounds really odd, since the stems are subjected to oxygen all the time. Why they’d suddenly start to oxidize and turn brown late in the season and not right from the beginning? As far as I’ve understood, wood oxidation would happen if you cut off some wood, so the underlying tissue gets exposed, in turn drying and oxidizing it. A completely different thing from lignification.

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked:
I make both 100% whole cluster (Ariane) and 100% destemmed (Colette) Pinot Noir (with Marie-Paule being a blend of the two). Same vineyard, very different wines. My original plan was to make less whole cluster on cooler vintages (due to less stem oxidation; by the way, the term “lignification” is misused, all mature plant tissue has lignification; the browning of stems is due to oxidation). But I am really, really liking my cooler climate whole cluster. It sings on many levels, changes dramatically after bottling (in a good way), and isn’t showing any green harshness or bitterness. To one of Marcus’ points, the destemmed Pinot is more predictable and well, boring from a wine maker’s point of view, but it does get lots of love from clients and critics.

I split my Sanford & Benedict today into two fermentation bins, one de-stemmed and one with some whole clusters at the bottom, as I processed today. Curious to see how they differ over time. Will keep them separate for as long as I can.

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked:
Not really trying to replicate anything, just seeing how they differ, with and without. If I like the bin with some whole cluster more in the end, I might up the percentage next year.

I do see one reason why some people like whole cluster more… it’s a lot prettier as you process it. I use perhaps the best destemmer I know of and it still opens up a lot of berries as it falls into tank. Meaning less chance to do carbonic, early. Already I suspect this may be one of the biggest pluses of whole cluster, if you can avoid the green… you get more “cascades” of fermentation and a longer one.

posts.cooked:

Your choice is a smart one, IMO. It’s, again in my opinion, better to walk up the stairs of using whole cluster slowly rather than trying to jump the whole set at once.

There are lots of pluses and minuses to whole cluster, but in my experience you either do it or you do not. And the reasons for either don’t mean a damn thing. You’ll either want to do it or you won’t.

And to be fair, we make a wee bit of Cab Franc. In year one it was 100% whole cluster, in year two it was 18%. It wasn’t green, it just wasn’t a good expression of the fruit(way too carbonic-y). The thin skins on PN burst faster, so you actually get less carbonic impact, but you do get cascades of fermentation.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I think most winemakers feel that stems ‘detract from the purity of the fruit that they receive’ - I would agree that they ‘detract’ but my goal in winemaking is not just to make ‘fruity’ wines but to build complexity in both flavors and aromas ‘organically’ and stems allow that to happen naturally.

I remember being offended when I tried an early Arnot-Roberts Peter Martin Ray Vyd. PN a number of years ago. Knowing that site so well from a few producers of quite a few vintages, and thinking it one of the best PN sites in CA, it did obscure what that vineyard had to give into an unrecognizable (but excellent) wine. It might be time to check in on a semi-mature bottle. Contrast that to my view on their Fellom Ranch Cab, as noted above. It showed the Montebello subregion quite well, which I love, and was compelling and inspiring.

Research, intuition, experimentation and results are good guides. We never did wc with PN because it didn’t fit with Bryan’s vision, and I don’t feel any of those (5-6/year, then down to two) PNs would’ve been better with it. But, we certainly utilized it with other grapes to good effect, imo, with the sites’ character readily apparent. Then, Rhys is well known for a good portion of stem inclusion, but they quickly determined a couple of the clones at Alpine didn’t well with it, so those got 100% destemmed from then on. But, those are components of a mostly wc wine.

Some grapes ask for it, others would suffer for it, and some can go either way. So, my view is do it when you should, not because you can.

Wes,

Is it possible to push techniques too far? Of course - that goes with ripeness levels, oak usage, maceration temperatures and times, and certainly stem inclusion. I find that with my wines, the stems tend to be more noticeable when young, but as the wine ages and matures, the stems ‘integrate’ and simply add ‘background notes’ rather than being at the foregound. There really is no one right or wrong answer here - and even your statement that some grapes ‘suffer for it’ is subjective, based on so many other factors.

To me, the key is alcohol levels, fermentation temperatures, and length of time in barrel . . .

Cheers

We weren’t reinventing the wheel, just being nimble and broadly aware. It was utilizing techniques, not pushing. It only looked ground-breaking some of the time because the industry standard approach is pushing every shape and sized peg through a single round hole. Bryan’s background was Pinot, with a gentle-handling, low-intervention approach. So, we were more about feeling out what various grapes had to offer, not trying to push them against their nature. If we were inspired to try something, we’d look for the right grapes to do it with. Bryan moved from 5-6 PNs a year to completely eliminating the grape, er, well, that was the intent, but good opportunities came up, so we were still making two from 2-3 vineyards. He never ever used wc on PN. Never thought it would be a good idea on any PN he got, and had no interest ever doing it with a PN of his. But, as someone generally opposed to wc in PN, he had no problem doing 100% wc with some other grapes.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
due to less stem oxidation; by the way, the term “lignification” is misused, all mature plant tissue has lignification; the browning of stems is due to oxidation

Can you give sources on this? Because at least I’ve seen Jamie Goode discuss all the time about stem lignification and I’d think he knows what he’s talking about, having PhD in plant biology and all.

Here’s a great explanation from Dr. Markus Keller, Washington State Univ (start at min 4:30 if you want to skip the intro… listen for at least 2 or 3 minutes…) 2021 Oregon Wine Symposium | Plant Biology – The Winter Basics | Biología vegetal - Conceptos básicos para el invierno - Oregon Wine Industry

Lignification of stems always comes up as a subject for whole cluster fermentations— the term in the wine world is used to indicate browning, which some believe will reduce green flavor components. In fact, plant cells produce lignin very early on for important functions such as water transport. See for example the basic Wikipedia explanation: Lignin - Wikipedia. From a science view, we should just say that we prefer stems that show browning (oxidation of dead cork cells)… But as you noted Otto, the term is so widely misused that this will not happen any time soon (Goode is just one example)!

WineBusiness Monthly - December 2018 Issue Article begins on page 40.

Have done experiments on this and found no evidence of flavor or number differences (that were knowable as being attributed to this as opposed to any other factor). We put the whole clusters on the top but the reasoning for that is 100% practical and has nothing to do with winemaking itself unless you count efficiency as an aspect of winemaking (you should).

Good overview of the Whole Bunch debate starting on page 27 in The Fine+Rare Burgundy Report
Gives perspectives from all sides.
https://www.frw.co.uk/media/burgundy-2019-report
The conclusion says it all.
“Whichever philosophy a vigneron chooses, there is
healthy debate in Burgundy today over whether or not to
use whole-bunch fermentation. It is just an extra nuance,
adding another layer of intrigue to this fascinating
fine wine region. The success of each of these different
approaches shows there is no right answer, but it does
make you wonder whether winemaking styles should be
playing more of a role when deciding which Burgundies
to buy – and whether vintage is as important as we
traditionally believe it to be.”

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
One more thing about that picture… I see a lot of berry variation. Many berries are lightly colored and translucent. Some are actually raisined. I bet that is another reason their Burgs are more complex than our Pinots. We may have an over-obsession in the USA with uniform ripeness in our fruit and clusters.

I’ve always wondered about this, and what Roy states is intuitive to me even if I don’t have a way to back it up with actual science. I think the culinary equivalent is the way I dice an onion when cooking. CIA graduates would cringe as I’m not really going for consistency/sameness. Some bits are a bit bigger/smaller. I get the majority of the onions properly browned, some a bit under-cooked and some a bit over (but not burnt) under the idea that it does give me more complexity of flavour. Not that I’m saying restaurants do it wrong, just that there are different drivers for what they are trying to do - they need to scale and keep consistency. Would be interesting if this is actually true and carried over to wine.
Good discussion by Etianne de Montille of his perspective on whole cluster in the back half of this video Domaine de Montille Virtual Tasting 22 July 2020 - YouTube
